VB
Work About Resume

Enterprise B2B · Multi-persona · Power Apps · Responsive

When 3,000 Volcafe employees were managing travel on WhatsApp messages and Excel sheets

Volcafe — one of the world's largest green coffee traders — had no structured way for employees to request, approve, or reconcile travel expenses. I designed a two-phase travel management system in Microsoft Power Apps, used daily across 14 countries.

Built on Microsoft Power Apps
My role
Lead UX Designer
Timeline
6 months
Platform
Power Apps (web + mobile)
Impact
↓ 70% manual effort
Client: Volcafe · ED&F Man Capital Markets · Internal enterprise tooling · Web + Mobile
01Design
strategy

The brief said "build a form".
The problem was a broken loop.

Volcafe employees travelling for business — client meetings, origin country visits, conferences — were managing travel approvals through email chains and WhatsApp messages. Finance tracked expenses in Excel. Approvers had no structured view of what was pending. The brief: "replace the Excel sheet." After research, the real problem was a two-phase accountability gap — proposed budgets and actual spends were managed in entirely separate systems with no connection between them.

The additional constraint: the solution had to be built in Microsoft Power Apps to integrate with existing Volcafe infrastructure (SharePoint, Teams, Outlook). This shaped every design decision — components had to work within Power Apps' capabilities while feeling as polished as a native web app.

01
Connect proposed and actual expenses
The core failure: budget approval and expense reconciliation were disconnected. One system, two phases, a visible link between what was planned and what was spent.
02
Serve two distinct roles without compromise
Employees need a fast, guided submission flow. Approvers need a clear overview with minimal clicks to approve or reject. The same underlying data, two completely different interfaces.
03
Work on mobile without compromise
Employees often submit requests while travelling. Approvers review on the go. Full parity between desktop and mobile — not a stripped-down mobile version.
04
Build a formal 6-stage audit trail
Finance and compliance needed every request traceable: Proposed → Submitted → Approved → Actual Expenses → Submitted → Approved. No stage skippable.
02Profile &
personas

Same trip.
Two completely different jobs to do.

Research across 18 interviews and 3 weeks of shadowing surfaced two primary personas with opposing mental models of what "a travel request" meant.

Persona A — The Employee
P. Sanjay
Operations Executive · Volcafe · India
Travels 4–8 times per year for business meets, origin visits, and conferences
Currently messages his manager on WhatsApp for travel approval before booking
Keeps expense receipts in a WhatsApp album and photos folder on his phone
Submits a final Excel with scanned receipts attached to email — often weeks after travel
Doesn't know if his request has been approved until he asks again
Submit the request and move on. Know that it's approved before booking. Not deal with it again until after the trip.
The post-trip reconciliation is the most dreaded part — reconstructing expenses from phone photos weeks later.
Persona B — The Approver
David Smith
Finance Manager · Volcafe · United Kingdom
Reviews 30–50 travel requests per month across multiple countries and divisions
Currently approves via email reply — no structured view of pending requests
Has to reconcile proposed budgets against actual costs manually after travel
No visibility into whether approved trips have been reconciled or are still outstanding
Responsible to finance leadership for budget compliance — but has no live budget tracking
Review quickly, approve confidently, track outstanding reconciliations without chasing people.
The gap between what was proposed and what was actually spent is invisible until someone sends the final Excel — often months later.
"I approved the trip. I have no idea what was actually spent until I chase him for the Excel — and that's usually after he's already left for the next one."
— David Smith, Finance Manager, during stakeholder interview
03Field
studies

Four weeks of research
before a single wireframe

Distributed workforce across India, UK, and Europe required remote and in-person methods. The most valuable insight didn't come from interviews — it came from watching a user reconstruct a 3-week trip from 47 phone photos.

◎
Contextual Inquiry
Shadowed 8 employees during post-trip expense submission. Observed the actual tools used: WhatsApp, personal notes, SMS from hotels. Documented the average time-to-submit: 3.4 hours per trip.
Output → Behavioural patterns, tool audit
◈
Stakeholder Interviews
18 interviews across employees, approvers, finance officers, and HR. Key finding: approvers and employees had 6 different definitions of what "approved" meant in the current process.
Output → Mental model map, definition conflicts
◆
Process Audit
Collected 3 months of travel emails, WhatsApp screenshots, and Excel files from 5 employees. Mapped every step in the current process. Found an average of 14 touchpoints per travel request — most of them manual.
Output → As-is process map, friction count
◇
Competitive Audit
Reviewed SAP Concur, Expensify, Zoho Expense, and TravelPerk. Benchmarked the proposed-vs-actual two-phase model — none of the reviewed tools handled it well for multi-currency, multi-country enterprise use.
Output → Feature gap analysis, pattern library
◉
Power Apps Constraints Audit
Critical phase unique to this project: 2 weeks mapping what Power Apps could and couldn't do. Determined which patterns required workarounds, which components had to be custom, and where mobile parity was non-trivial.
Output → Technical constraint map, design boundaries
◐
Usability Testing (2 rounds)
Round 1: Paper prototype testing with 6 employees and 3 approvers. Validated the two-phase model and the 6-stage lifecycle. Round 2: High-fidelity Power Apps prototype, 8 users, both personas, full end-to-end flows.
Output → Task completion rates, navigation fixes
04Data
gathered

Numbers that
changed the design direction

Four findings from research that each directly changed a design decision.

14
Manual touchpoints per travel request in the old process
WhatsApp message → verbal approval → email → Excel → scanned receipt → email attachment. 14 handoffs per trip, most of them invisible to finance.
3.4h
Average time to complete post-trip expense reconciliation
Employees spent 3.4 hours on average reconstructing expenses after travel. Most of that time was hunting for receipts — not filling in numbers.
6
Different definitions of "approved" across the organisation
Verbal, WhatsApp message, email reply, manager signature, finance stamp, and system flag. No shared definition. This directly drove the 6-stage lifecycle design.
100%
Of approvers had zero live visibility into outstanding reconciliations
Not a single approver could tell us how many trips they'd approved were still awaiting actual expense submission. The data existed — it was just completely invisible.
"I took the photos during the trip thinking I'd deal with them later. 'Later' was three weeks after I got back, and I couldn't remember what half of them were for."
— P. Sanjay, Operations Executive, during contextual inquiry session
05Task
analysis

What each persona
actually does, step by step

Task decomposition across both phases of the lifecycle. The two-phase model was the core design insight — most existing tools treated expense submission as a single event. This system makes the gap between proposed and actual a first-class concept.

Employee — P. Sanjay (Phase 1 + 2)
  • 1
    Create new travel request
    Fill destination, dates, purpose, opening balance medium (cash/card/both).
  • 2
    Add proposed expenses
    Category + amount + description per expense item. Save as Draft or Submit for Approval.
    Pain (old): Estimates were informal — no formal record before approval.
  • 3
    Track approval status
    Dashboard shows Pending Approval, Approved, Rejected, Action Required statuses.
  • 4
    Submit actual expenses (Phase 2)
    After travel — add expenses by date. Category, amount, currency, payment medium, attach receipts.
    Pain (old): Reconstructed from phone photos weeks after travel.
  • 5
    Submit actual expenses for approval
    Review total actual vs proposed. Submit. View proposed/actual/balance comparison.
  • 6
    Receive final approval or respond to rejection
    Edit and resubmit if rejected. Track closure.
Approver — David Smith (Phase 1 + 2)
  • 1
    Review incoming travel requests
    Card grid dashboard with status filter tabs: All, Pending Approval, Approved, Rejected.
    Pain (old): Email inbox with no prioritisation or status grouping.
  • 2
    Review proposed expenses
    Open request — see employee details, destination, dates, purpose, itemised expenses with total proposed.
  • 3
    Approve or reject Phase 1
    One-tap Approve or Reject. Status updated, employee notified.
  • 4
    Review actual expenses (Phase 2)
    See date-based expense breakdown. Proposed vs actual comparison with balance shown. Receipts attached and viewable.
  • 5
    Approve or reject actual expenses
    Final approval closes the request. Reject returns to employee for correction.
  • 6
    Track outstanding reconciliations
    Dashboard shows pending actual expense submissions. No more manual chasing.
Artifact — Journey maps
Before and after — the travel expense experience
As-is: 14 touchpoints, 3.4 hours of reconciliation, zero visibility. To-be: one system, two phases, full audit trail.
As-is journey — the WhatsApp-to-Excel process
Stage 1Pre-trip request
Stage 2Approval (informal)
Stage 3Travel
Stage 4Post-trip recon
Stage 5Finance closure
Employee
Messages manager on WhatsApp with travel dates and rough budget. Sometimes emails HR.
Gets verbal or WhatsApp confirmation. Books travel. No formal record.
Takes photos of receipts on phone. Keeps notes in personal app. Some receipts lost.
Reconstructs expenses from 47 phone photos. Fills Excel. Attaches scans to email. 3.4 hours.
Finance receives Excel. Cross-references with verbal approval. Updates budget sheet. Takes 2–3 weeks.
Approver
Receives WhatsApp or email. No structured view of pending requests.
Replies "OK" or verbally approves. No record created.
Has no visibility into what's happening.
Receives Excel attachment. Manually reconciles against memory of what was approved.
Updates personal budget tracker. No system-of-record. Process repeats.
Emotion
Neutral
Uncertain — is this approved?
Stressed about receipts
Very frustrated — wasted hours
Resigned — this is just how it works
To-be journey — the redesigned TMS experience
Stage 1Create request
Stage 2Approval (Phase 1)
Stage 3Travel
Stage 4Actual expenses
Stage 5Final approval
Employee
Fills guided form — destination, dates, purpose, proposed items. Save as Draft or submit. Works on mobile.
Tracks status on dashboard. Notified on approval. Books travel with formal confirmation.
Adds expenses daily on mobile — category, amount, attach receipt photo immediately. No post-trip reconstruction.
Reviews date-based breakdown. Sees proposed vs actual comparison. Submits for final approval.
Receives closure notification. Request archived with full audit trail.
Design solution: daily mobile expense entry eliminates post-trip reconstruction entirely
Approver
Dashboard shows all requests with status filters. Clear pending count. Card layout for quick scanning.
Reviews itemised proposed expenses. One-tap approve or reject. Status updates automatically.
Passive visibility — can see which trips are active. No action required.
Reviews actual vs proposed with receipt attachments. Balance shown prominently.
One-tap final approval. Complete audit trail for finance. Outstanding reconciliations tracked automatically.
Design solution: proposed/actual comparison makes budget variance visible at a glance
Emotion
Guided, calm
Design solution: structured form with inline guidance
Confident — clear confirmation
In control — logging as they go
Efficient — already done
Satisfied, complete

Power Apps constraint note
All components designed within Power Apps limits — no external libraries. Custom gallery patterns for the accordion and card grid.
09Final
design
All screens — shipped product
Desktop + mobile, both phases
20 screens across 2 roles, 2 phases, and 2 breakpoints. Key decision: full mobile parity — not a stripped version.
Employee dashboard — card grid with status filter tabs. "Create New Request" prominent. All (8), Draft, Submitted (5), Action Required (1), Approved (2), Rejected.
Approver dashboard — same card layout, different data. Pending Approval count drives urgency. Submission date visible for prioritisation.
New travel request — split layout: travel details left, proposed expenses right. Inline add-expense form with category, description, amount. Running total visible.
Approver — Phase 1 review — all travel details and proposed expense breakdown. Approve / Reject / Cancel. One action, one screen.
Employee — actual expenses form — date selector drives the accordion. Opening Balance vs Actual Expenses strip shows budget position at a glance.
Approver — Phase 2 final review — actual vs proposed comparison, date-based breakdown, receipt links. Approve / Reject / Cancel.
Mobile screens — full parity with desktop
Employee dashboard — search + filter + card list
New request — 6-step stepper, stacked form
Approver review — proposed expenses + approve/reject
Actual expenses — date accordion, add expense, submit
Approved request view — read-only, stage 3 confirmed
Approver dashboard — alternate card design — proposed expenses and actual expenses shown side by side on each card. Budget variance visible without opening the request. This was the version shipped after usability testing revealed approvers wanted to see both figures at the card level.
↓ 70%
Reduction in manual effort per travel request — from 14 touchpoints to 4
↓ 90%
Post-trip reconciliation time — from 3.4 hours average to under 20 minutes
3,000+
Employees across 14 countries using the system on web and mobile
Section 10
What I'd
do differently
"The Power Apps constraint was underestimated at the start. We designed the ideal experience first, then hit the platform ceiling during build. Some components had to be rebuilt twice — once for what we wanted, once for what Power Apps could actually do."

The biggest mistake was sequencing: we ran the UX process as if we were building on any platform, then handed designs to the Power Apps developer. The platform constraint audit should have been a design input, not a development problem. Three features — the date-range filter on the approver dashboard, the inline receipt preview, and the notification system — required significant rework because of Power Apps limitations we discovered too late.

The second thing I'd change: we underweighted the approver experience in early testing. We had 6 employee testers and only 2 approvers in round 1. The card grid dashboard design was iterated twice after launch because approvers with large volumes (50+ requests) found the grid hard to scan. A table view option was added in v1.1 — it should have been in v1.0.

What I'm proud of: insisting on full mobile parity. The stakeholders pushed for "mobile-lite" to save development time. I showed data that 60% of employees would submit requests on mobile during or immediately after travel. We built full parity. Post-launch, 68% of actual expense submissions came from mobile. The data was the argument again.
